name: Pawpaw
summary: The sole larval host plant of the Zebra Swallowtail butterfly. Pittsburgh-region restoration plantings documented the swallowtail's return to formerly extirpated areas in 2024 after pawpaws were re-established — a near-direct analog for what's possible in NEPA.
notes: Native to PA; hardy Zone 5–8. Patch-forming small understory tree (15–30 ft). Maroon spring flowers are fly-pollinated (carrion-mimic). Large green fruit ripens in September — tastes like banana-mango custard; eaten by raccoons, opossums, foxes, deer (post-ripening only — unripe fruit is bitter). Acetogenin chemistry makes pawpaw essentially deer-proof through its entire life cycle.

refs.label: PublicSource — Zebra Swallowtail return to Pittsburgh

planting.aspect: Moist coves; bottomland; sheltered cool sites
planting.light: Partial shade preferred; tolerates full sun once established
planting.moisture: Mesic to moist; well-drained but not dry
planting.soil: Rich, organic, slightly acidic to neutral
planting.site_notes: Self-incompatible — plant at least 2 unrelated trees within 30 ft for cross-pollination and fruit set. No deer protection needed (chemical defense). Plant in coves and bottomland — not exposed ridges.
